# Paneflow on Windows

> Paneflow ships a **signed native Windows `.msi`** (since v0.5.0). WSL2 + WSLg with the Linux build remains a fully supported alternative.

Canonical: https://paneflow.dev/docs/installation/windows

<!-- Auto-generated mirror of paneflow.dev - do not edit by hand.
     Source of truth: the paneflow.dev docs. Regenerated by
     scripts/sync-public-docs.ts in the site repo. -->

Paneflow now ships a **native Windows installer** - a `.msi`
code-signed by **StriveX** via Azure Trusted Signing - starting with
**v0.5.0**. The native build is in a best-effort rollout while the
Windows CI leg is hardened; if you hit a packaging issue, the
**WSL2 + WSLg** path with the Linux build is a fully supported
fallback.

  **TL;DR.** Download `paneflow-<version>-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.msi`
  from [GitHub releases](https://github.com/ArthurDEV44/paneflow/releases/latest)
  and run it. On first launch Microsoft Defender SmartScreen may show
  **"Windows protected your PC"** - this is a *reputation* prompt for a
  new publisher, **not** a signing failure. Click **More info → Run
  anyway**; the publisher reads **StriveX**. Prefer Linux tooling? The
  WSL2 path further down still works.

## Installing the native Windows build [#installing-the-native-windows-build]

1. Open the
   [latest release](https://github.com/ArthurDEV44/paneflow/releases/latest)
   and download the Windows installer:

   ```
   paneflow-<version>-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.msi
   ```

2. Double-click the `.msi`. If SmartScreen interrupts with **"Windows
   protected your PC"**, see
   [the next section](#windows-protected-your-pc-on-first-launch) - the
   installer is signed; that prompt is expected for a new publisher.

3. Follow the installer. Paneflow installs as a standard Windows app and
   appears in the Start menu.

Requirements: Windows 10 or 11 (x86_64). A GPU with Vulkan support is
recommended - GPUI renders through the GPU.

## "Windows protected your PC" on first launch [#windows-protected-your-pc-on-first-launch]

When you run the installer, Microsoft Defender SmartScreen may show:

> **Windows protected your PC**
> Microsoft Defender SmartScreen prevented an unrecognized app from
> starting. Running this app might put your PC at risk.

**This is expected, and it does *not* mean the installer is unsigned or
unsafe.** SmartScreen runs two *independent* checks:

1. **Signature** - is the file validly signed and untampered? Paneflow's
   `.msi` **passes**: it is Authenticode-signed by **StriveX** (via
   Azure Trusted Signing) and timestamped.
2. **Reputation** - has Microsoft already seen this exact file/publisher
   downloaded widely enough to vouch for it? For a **brand-new publisher
   identity**, the answer starts at *not yet* - so SmartScreen shows the
   generic "unrecognized app" prompt **even though the signature is
   valid**. The "downloaded from the internet" tag (Mark-of-the-Web) is
   what triggers the check in the first place.

The dialog you see is verdict **#2**, not #1. Reputation accrues
automatically with downloads and time - Microsoft's rule of thumb is
roughly **3,000 unique downloads or 6-8 weeks** of consistent signing
with the same identity, after which the prompt stops appearing. (An EV
certificate would *not* skip this: since March 2024 Microsoft treats OV
and EV identically for reputation build-up.)

### Run the installer anyway [#run-the-installer-anyway]

1. In the SmartScreen dialog, click **More info**
   *(French Windows: "Informations complémentaires")*.
2. Confirm the **Publisher** line now reads **StriveX**, then click
   **Run anyway** *(French: "Exécuter quand même")*.

If the publisher does **not** read StriveX, stop - do not run the file,
and re-download it from the official
[releases page](https://github.com/ArthurDEV44/paneflow/releases/latest).

### Verify the signature yourself [#verify-the-signature-yourself]

Right-click the `.msi` → **Properties** → **Digital Signatures** tab.
The signer should be **StriveX** (SHA-256, timestamped).

Or from PowerShell:

```powershell
Get-AuthenticodeSignature .\paneflow-<version>-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.msi |
  Format-List Status, SignerCertificate
```

`Status` must be `Valid` and the certificate subject must contain
`O=StriveX`. With the Windows SDK installed, you can also run:

```powershell
signtool verify /pa /v paneflow-<version>-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.msi
```

which must print `Successfully verified` and a certificate chain
anchoring to `O=StriveX`.

## Frequently asked questions about Paneflow on Windows [#frequently-asked-questions-about-paneflow-on-windows]

### Is there a native Windows build? [#is-there-a-native-windows-build]

Yes - a signed `.msi` since **v0.5.0**. It is code-signed by StriveX via
**Azure Trusted Signing** (a Public-Trust certificate profile - no EV
certificate is required). The native build is currently a best-effort
rollout while the Windows CI leg is promoted from best-effort to
hard-required over the next few releases. Until reputation builds up,
expect the SmartScreen prompt described
[above](#windows-protected-your-pc-on-first-launch); use the WSL2 path
below if you hit a packaging issue.

### Does Paneflow work with Windows Terminal? [#does-paneflow-work-with-windows-terminal]

Paneflow is itself a terminal workspace - you do not run it *inside*
Windows Terminal, you run it *instead of* Windows Terminal. Windows
Terminal hosts traditional command-line shells (PowerShell, `cmd.exe`,
WSL bash); Paneflow is a graphical multi-pane workspace built for
orchestrating CLI coding agents. On Windows you can run Paneflow either
as a native app (the signed `.msi` above) or via WSL2 + WSLg.

### How do I run Paneflow on Windows with WSL2 instead? [#how-do-i-run-paneflow-on-windows-with-wsl2]

The WSL2 + WSLg path is a fully supported alternative - handy if you
already live in WSL2 (Linux toolchains, Linux paths, `apt`-installed
agents). It takes about five minutes on Windows 11.

1. **Enable WSL2** (if not already installed). Open PowerShell as
   Administrator:

   ```powershell
   wsl --install
   ```

   This installs WSL2 + the default Ubuntu distribution. Reboot when
   prompted.

2. **Update WSLg** (ships by default on Windows 11; on Windows 10 you
   need to install it manually - see the
   [Microsoft WSLg docs](https://github.com/microsoft/wslg)).

3. **Install Paneflow's Linux `.deb`** inside the WSL2 distribution:

   ```bash
   curl -LO https://github.com/ArthurDEV44/paneflow/releases/latest/download/paneflow-x86_64.deb
   sudo apt install ./paneflow-x86_64.deb
   paneflow
   ```

   The Paneflow window opens as a native Windows window via WSLg's
   Wayland forwarding. GPU acceleration works via the WSLg Mesa driver.

### Will the Linux WSL2 install still work now that the native build ships? [#will-the-linux-wsl2-install-still-work]

Yes. The Linux build remains available indefinitely - WSL2 is a
first-class Linux host, and any Paneflow Linux release can run there.
You can choose between running Paneflow as a native Windows app or as a
WSL2/WSLg app. Most users will want the native build for tighter Windows
integration (Windows notifications, Windows file picker, native title
bar), but WSL2 remains a great path for users who already do most of
their work inside WSL2.
